coredata.dc:description: Despite significant oncology advancements over recent decades, cancer remains a leading global cause of death. Theranostic strategies for targeted delivery of chemotherapeutic agents and imaging agents are gaining traction due to their immense potential. Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly nanographene oxide (NGO), have emerged as promising candidates for drug delivery and bioimaging, owing to their biocompatibility, versatile photophysical properties, and tunable size, shape, and surface chemistry. Additionally, their responsiveness to stimuli such as pH and light enhances their suitability for controlled drug release. In this study, we successfully synthesized and extruded NGO (for size homogenization), followed by thorough characterization and conjugation with doxorubicin (DOX), a widely used chemotherapeutic agent. NGO exhibits green fluorescence and a negative surface charge due to its oxygen-rich functional groups, which are ionizable depending on pH. Notably, NGO causes less membrane destabilization compared to other materials, while demonstrating a higher drug loading capacity despite a relatively low fluorescence quantum yield of 19.69 %. Importantly, drug release can be triggered by pH changes and laser irradiation. Our conjugates enhance fluorescent detection of cellular uptake, establishing them as effective theranostic tools. Cytotoxicity assessments conducted both in vitro and in vivo reveal that NGO@DOX conjugates exhibit greater toxicity compared to commercial carbon quantum dots (CQD) conjugated with DOX, attributed to a more favorable drug release profile influenced by the unique surface chemistry of NGO. This study underscores the potential of NGO as nanocarriers for theranostic applications, seamlessly integrating therapeutic efficacy with imaging capabilities and paving the way for more effective cancer treatments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of extrusion pos-production procedure in the size, morphology and surface charge of NGO. (A) Hydrodynamic size and zeta-potential values of NGO before and after extrusion evaluated by dynamic and electrophoretic light scattering; (B) Size distribution of NGO before and after extrusion evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM); (C) SEM images of NGO before extrusion; (D) SEM images of NGO after extrusion.

Optical properties of NGO. UV–Vis absorbance (A), and fluorescence excitation and emission (B) spectra of NGO extruded through different pore size filters (50, 100, 200, 400 nm). Photographs show the color of NGO under visible light (A) or green emission under excitation at 461 nm (B).

pH impact in NGO surface functional groups. Variation at different pH values of NGO's maximum wavelength of emission (green symbols) (A) or NGO's ZP values (B) and comparison with CQD (blue symbols). Schematic representation of the effect of pH in NGO surface functional groups ionization (C). Regions 1, 2 and 3 are assigned to the pH intervals where the surface groups represented can be found. ATR-FTIR spectrum of NGO in the region of 1700 to 1300 cm−1 at pH 5.5 and 7.4. The dashed lines assign the antisymmetric (υas) and symmetric (υs) stretch vibrations attributed to carboxyl (COOH) and carboxylate (COO−) moieties (D).

Conjugation of NGO and DOX. UV–Vis absorbance spectra free DOX (red), NGO (green), sum of NGO and DOX spectra (NGO + DOX dashed purple) or DOX loaded NGO (NGO@DOX purple) (A). Dynamics of DOX binding to NGO at two pH values, represented as variations in ZP (ΔZP), measured by ELS, as a function of increasing DOX concentrations added to an NGO fixed concentration (B). DOX dissociation constants, Kd (C) and loading efficiency, DL% (D), determined at two pH values, for NGO (green bars) and CQD (blue bars).

Interaction with lipid membrane models. SAXS patterns of DPPC in the fluid phase (50 °C) at pH 5.5 (A) and pH 7.4 (B) interacting with: free DOX (red), NGO (green), CQD (blue), DOX loaded NGO (NGO@DOX green dashed) and DOX loaded CQD (CQD@DOX blue dashed).

DOX stimuli-triggered release. DOX release profile from NGO at pH 5.5 (green dashed curve) and pH 7.4 (green curve) and respective mathematic fitting models (A). Raman spectra of NGO, DOX, NGO@DOX and NGO after laser exposure and DOX-triggered release (B).

Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity. MTT assay (A–F) was performed to evaluate the impact of conjugates synthesized with CQD (A–C) or NGO (D–F) on the metabolic viability of L929 cells after 24 (A and D), 48 (B and E) or 72 (C and F) h of incubation. With Hoechst assay (G–J) the effect of conjugates synthesized with CQD (G and H) or NGO (I and J) on cell proliferation after 24 (G and I) or 48 (H and J) h of incubation was evaluated. Intracellular DOX and CQD/NGO@DOX uptake by L929 cells after 4 h exposure (K). One-Way ANOVA was performed. The data is shown as mean ± SD. p ≤ 0.05: ∗, p ≤ 0.01: ∗∗, p ≤ 0.001: ∗∗∗, p ≤ 0.0001: ∗∗∗∗.

Heart rate, survival and biodistribution assessment: Cumulative heart rate (A and C) and survival (B and D) of zebrafish embryos exposed to CQD@DOX (A and B) and NGO@DOX (C and D). DOX uptake by zebrafish embryos after 72 h-treatment, evaluated through fluorescence microscopy. Scale bar in microscopic images: 500 μm (E–G). (E) brighfield; (F) TRITC; and (G): overlay. Mix model two-way ANOVA was performed for heart rate. The data is shown as mean ± SD. p ≤ 0.05: ∗, p ≤ 0.01: ∗∗, p ≤ 0.001: ∗∗∗, p ≤ 0.0001: ∗∗∗∗.
Optical properties comparison between NGO and CQD. FWHM: full width at half maximum.
Long distance (d) and correlation length (ξ) determined from SAXS diffraction patterns, at 50 °C, at pH 5.5 and 7.4.
Metabolic viability of BJ-5ta, MCF10A and MDA-MB-231 cells after 24, 48 or 72 h of incubation with CQD@DOX, NGO@DOX, CQD, NGO or DOX.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: One-Way ANOVA was performed. The data is shown as mean ± SD. p ≤ 0.05: ∗, p ≤ 0.01: ∗∗, p ≤ 0.001: ∗∗∗, p ≤ 0.0001: ∗∗∗∗. n.s.: not significant and (−): not applicable.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite significant oncology advancements over recent decades, cancer remains a leading global cause of death. Theranostic strategies for targeted delivery of chemotherapeutic agents and imaging agents are gaining traction due to their immense potential. Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly nanographene oxide (NGO), have emerged as promising candidates for drug delivery and bioimaging, owing to their biocompatibility, versatile photophysical properties, and tunable size, shape, and surface chemistry. Additionally, their responsiveness to stimuli such as pH and light enhances their suitability for controlled drug release. In this study, we successfully synthesized and extruded NGO (for size homogenization), followed by thorough characterization and conjugation with doxorubicin (DOX), a widely used chemotherapeutic agent. NGO exhibits green fluorescence and a negative surface charge due to its oxygen-rich functional groups, which are ionizable depending on pH. Notably, NGO causes less membrane destabilization compared to other materials, while demonstrating a higher drug loading capacity despite a relatively low fluorescence quantum yield of 19.69 %. Importantly, drug release can be triggered by pH changes and laser irradiation. Our conjugates enhance fluorescent detection of cellular uptake, establishing them as effective theranostic tools. Cytotoxicity assessments conducted both in vitro and in vivo reveal that NGO@DOX conjugates exhibit greater toxicity compared to commercial carbon quantum dots (CQD) conjugated with DOX, attributed to a more favorable drug release profile influenced by the unique surface chemistry of NGO. This study underscores the potential of NGO as nanocarriers for theranostic applications, seamlessly integrating therapeutic efficacy with imaging capabilities and paving the way for more effective cancer treatments.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Attenuated total reflectance coupled with Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy
Carbon quantum dots and doxorubicin conjugates

ce:section.ce:para: Around the world, cancer remains a leading cause of death and permanent disability [1,2]. Together with the prevalence of cancer risk factors, many of which linked to socioeconomic development, the rapid rise in incidence and mortality is a reflection of population growth and aging [3–5]. Consequently estimates of a 77 % increase in the number of new cancer cases between 2022 and 2050 urge the development of new therapeutical approaches [5].

The rapid advancement of nanotechnology, and nanocarriers in particular, shows great promise for cancer treatment. Moreover, multifunctional nanosystems are believed to improve therapeutic efficiency by facilitating tumor targeting [6].

Different therapy strategies have been applied to cancer treatment. However, the methods available in the clinic face major throwbacks, in terms of toxicity, specificity, risk of recurrence and development of resistance [7]. Carbon-based nanomaterials (CBN) have garnered considerable interest for their potential to address these limitations [2,8,9]. CBN are readily functionalized and can be used in drug delivery, gene therapy, cancer imaging, and theranostics [2]. Among CBN, graphene oxide-based nanomaterials—referred to as nanographene oxide (NGO)— and carbon quantum dots (CQD) stand out due to their unique properties [10–20]. NGO consists of one to few layers of sp2-bonded carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, with sp³ domains linked to oxygen functional groups. This structure endows NGO with exceptional versatility, high aspect ratio, and distinct optical characteristics, including non-blinking photoluminescence and photostability [10–12]. The high density of oxygen-rich functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxide groups), facilitate easy functionalization with organic molecules or biological structures through π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions, enabling various applications under mild conditions [10–12]. Although NGO exhibits a lower fluorescence quantum yield compared to heavy metal-based quantum dots, its higher drug loading capacity and biocompatibility, combined with its physicochemical properties and intrinsic photoluminescence enable simultaneous drug delivery and bioimaging applications [10–12].

Despite doxorubicin (DOX) being a powerful chemotherapeutic with broad spectrum of antineoplastic activity that kills fast-growing cancer cells, it has a major drawback of displaying toxicity in healthy tissues, particularly the heart [21,22]. This constitutes its most important risk in conventional clinical treatments. Nevertheless, DOX-based therapeutics are approved to treat several types of cancer, either alone or in combination with other drugs [22,23].

Stimulus-responsive drug delivery nanosystems, developed in recent years as a strategy for triggering drug release on-site, such as NGO, may be determinant to maximize DOX therapeutic value while reducing the toxicity bias. The triggering strategy promotes drug release limited to the target tissue level (e.g., release triggered by a pH stimulus in tumor tissues with a more acidic environment) or focused on the intracellular level (e.g., release triggered by acidic pH of lysosomes). Indeed, in tumor tissue, rapid cell proliferation and metabolic demands lead to a high rate of glycolysis and consequent lactate accumulation, which promotes a reduction in the pH values of 5.5–6.8 in the tumor microenvironment. This contrasts with the pH found in normal tissues and in the blood (≈7.4) [24–28]. External stimuli (such as light) introduced into the pathological tissues from outside the body can also facilitate the on-site drug-triggered release [29].

The current study takes a novel approach to produce more uniformly sized NGO and conjugate it with DOX (NGO@DOX), taking great care in their physical, chemical and biophysical characterization to explore its potential for both therapeutic and imaging applications [30,31]. The NGO was produced using a top-down approach and subsequently extruded to achieve uniform size. The conjugation with DOX aims to facilitate drug accumulation in target tissues in a more controlled manner [26,32] through pH-responsive release mechanisms.

Commercial CQD, used as reference, did not have a detailed characterization available, so CQD and NGO were thoroughly studied regarding fluorescence and absorbance behavior prior to their conjugation with DOX. Drug dissociation constant (Kd) from the conjugates and drug loading (DL%) were determined, and a toxicological profile for these new conjugates was constructed using several mammalian cell lines and fish acute embryotoxicity (FET) assays on zebrafish (Danio rerio) embryos as recommended by OECD test guideline (TG) 236 [33].

While NGO by itself showed no cytotoxicity, DOX conjugation increased cytotoxicity. The type of nanomaterial used—either CQD or NGO—significantly influenced DOX toxicity due to differences in surface chemistry and interactions with the drug. The Kd value for CQD was considerably lower than that for NGO, indicating that DOX release was more restricted from the commercial dots. FET assays confirmed the expected DOX-associated cardiotoxicity, while cell viability assays showed that the type of graphene-based material impacted drug release and activity. Overall, NGO demonstrated a variety of advantageous properties for drug delivery in cancer therapy, including high drug loading capacity, a reasonable fluorescence quantum yield, and green fluorescence emission. These features enhance the detection of cellular uptake and enable pH- and laser-triggered release. Additionally, the biocompatibility of NGO positions it as a highly effective theranostic platform for cancer treatment, offering a complementary approach to well-established drug delivery systems such as polymeric micelles and liposomes.

NGO were prepared by a top-down approach from carbon black by chemical oxidation with some modifications of previously reported methodology [34]. In brief, 0.4 g of Vulcan CX-72 carbon black was refluxed for 24 h in 200 mL of HNO3 (6 M) for 24 h. The suspension was allowed to undergo natural cooling to reach room temperature, after which it was subjected to a resting period of no less than 10 h. This process facilitated the separation of the supernatant and sediment components. The supernatant was collected and heated to 120 °C to remove the water and nitric acid through evaporation. This process yielded a reddish-brown solid which was freeze-dried until further use. To improve size uniformity of the NGO synthesized a post processing method was used. An aliquot of the solid was resuspended in ultrapure water (0.5 mg/mL) and the aqueous suspension containing a wide variety of sizes of GO nanosheets was sequentially extruded at room temperature through polycarbonate membrane filters (Nucleopore membrane filters, VWR, Radnor, USA) of decreasing pore sizes of 400 nm, 200 nm, 100 nm and 50 nm (10 passages each) using a 10 mL Lipex™ extruder (Northern Lipids, Burnaby, Canada) at 6–8 bar.

Several methods were used to characterize the produced NGO. The surface topography, morphology and diameters distribution of the NGO were evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). 50 μL of ethanolic dispersions of NGO (before and after extrusion process) were applied in an Au substrate and dried under a nitrogen stream following a reported protocol [35]. The substrate was mounted with a double-sided conductive adhesive tape on a sample stud. SEM images were obtained on a Phenom ProX equipment (Paralab, Gondomar, Portugal) with an acceleration voltage of 5 kV, in charge reduction mode and a backscatter detection. Images were taken on a scale of 3 μm and an image analysis software, Digimizer® (version 4.6.1, MedCalc Software), was used for manual and accurate measurement of the NGO diameters (calculated from the measurements of at least 500 NGO platelets). Size distribution of NGO's aqueous dispersion (before and after extrusion process) was also evaluated by Dynamic light (DLS) using the Anton Paar Litesizer® 500 (Anton Paar GmbH, Graz, Austria) using an Omega cuvette Mat. No. 225288 (Anton Paar GmbH) at 25 ± 1 °C. Size was obtained from the correlogram using Anton Paar Litesizer® 500 software (Anton Paar GmbH) after cumulant analysis according to ISO 22412:2008 [36]. Photoluminescence and physical-chemical properties were evaluated through absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy, quantum yield (QY) determination, zeta potential (ZP) determination by electrophoretic light scattering (ELS), confocal Raman spectroscopy and attenuated total reflectance coupled with Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy.

Absorption spectra of aqueous dispersions of CQD and NGO (1 mg/mL) were recorded with a double-beam Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus UV–Vis–NIR (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) spectrophotometer within a 250–700 nm range. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQD and NGO's aqueous or pH buffered dispersions (universal buffer prepared following a reported protocol [37] that allows attaining pH values from 1 to 12) were acquired in a fluorolog-QM fluorimeter (HORIBA SAS, Longjumeau, France). For each pH the emission spectra of NGO or CQD were taken for a range of wavelengths from 460 nm to 685 nm. The excitation varied from 350 nm to 450 nm in steps of 10 nm. Acquisition of the emission spectra was made using a low scanning speed and integration time of 1s.

The fluorescent quantum yield of NGO and CQD was calculated using the comparative William's method [38]. Quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was employed as a reference and the quantum yield was calculated according to equation (1):
Q Y

=
F s
A r

F r
A s
where, Fs and Fr correspond to the integrated fluorescence emission of the sample and reference, respectively. As and Ar are the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the sample and reference, respectively. QYs is the quantum yield of the sample, and QYr is the quantum yield of the reference (
Q Y
r
= 54 %). The quantum yield of both CQD and NGO was determined at an excitation wavelength of 335 nm [39].

ZP measurements of CQD and NGO in pH buffered dispersions were carried out like the procedure described for size measurements, but in ELS mode, where ZP results were obtained by converting electrophoretic mobility using the Helmholtz-von Smoluchowski method [40].

Raman scattering experiments of NGO aqueous dispersion were performed at room temperature (20 °C) in a back scattering geometry on alpha300 R confocal Raman microscope (WITec, Ulm, Germany). It was used a 1.5 mW excitation 532 nm Nd:YAG laser, focused on the sample by a × 50 lens (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). The spectra were first collected with a 600 groove per mm grating using 10 acquisitions with a 2 s acquisition time.

Sample preparation for ATR-FTIR spectroscopy involved deposition of 50 μL of NGO buffered dispersion (1 mg/mL) on a small crucible of aluminum foil, followed by stove drying at 40 °C. Measurements were taken using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Spectrum Two™ FTIR PerkinElmer, Waltham, USA) by pressing the aluminum foil against the ATR accessory's crystal. FTIR spectra were obtained from 400 to 4000 cm−1, with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 64 scans per spectrum.

CQD/NGO@DOX conjugates were prepared by incubation of DOX aqueous solution with CQD or NGO aqueous dispersion. Conjugation was based on electrostatic attraction between DOX positively charged amino groups and CQD/NGO negatively charged carboxyl groups. Final CQD/NGO@DOX conjugates contained concentrations of 0.1 mg/mL of CQD/NGO and 8 × 10−4 M of DOX.

To assess the pH dependence of CQD/NGO@DOX conjugates formation and determine the binding parameters, CQD or NGO buffered dispersions (pH 5.5 and pH 7.4) were incubated with increasing amounts of DOX to obtain CQD/NGO@DOX conjugates containing concentrations of 0.2 mg/mL of CQD/NGO and 0 to 3 × 10−4 M of DOX. The ZP of the CQD/NGO@DOX conjugates was measured by ELS as previously described and ΔZP was calculated as the variation of ZP between the free CQD/NGO and the conjugates CQD/NGO@DOX. The experimental values (ΔZP as a function of DOX concentration [DOX]) were fitted according to the Langmuir binding (equation (2)) [41]:
Δ Z P =

Multilamellar lipid vesicles (MLV) were prepared through lipid film hydration method. 9 mg of dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC; COATSOME, NOF America corporation, New York, USA) were solubilized in a 3:1 mixture of chloroform and methanol, respectively. Solvents were evaporated under a nitrogen stream, forming the lipid film. The lipid film was placed in a desiccator overnight to ensure complete dryness. Lipid film hydration was performed with 90 μL of buffer at pH 5.5 and 7.4 alone or containing either DOX (3.4 × 10−4 M), CQD (0.2 mg/mL), NGO (0.2 mg/mL) or conjugates with CQD or NGO and DOX (CQD/NGO@DOX). After alternate heating cycles and vortex agitation, MLV were produced. MLV were transferred to X-ray transparent boron-silicate capillaries that were centrifuged at 3000 rpm (15 min) and flame sealed to form multilamellar stacking lipid models at the bottom of the capillaries. SAXS experiments were conducted in Elettra Sincrotrone Trieste's in-house instrumentation, which included a Pilatus 3 1 M detector (SAXS).

Dialysis was used to evaluate in vitro pH-responsive DOX release. NGO@DOX conjugates (1 mL containing 3.4 × 10−4 M DOX and 0.2 mg/mL of CQD/NGO) were introduced into dialysis membranes (Float-A-Lyzer®, 3.5 kD, VWR International) submerged in 40 mL of dissolving media to create sink conditions. The dissolution medium is made up of buffer solutions that are used to mimic the pH values found in vivo: pH 5.5 mimics the more acidic conditions found in tumor microenvironment tissues, specifically endosomal vesicles, and pH 7.4 mimics the physiological environment of normal tissues or plasma [24–28]. The system was maintained at 37 ± 1 °C with orbital stirring (IKA® KS 3000 control, Staufen, Germany) at 250 rpm to approximate body temperature. At predetermined intervals, 1.0 mL of each sample was collected and replaced with 1.0 mL of fresh dissolving medium until the experiment was completed in 24 h. The amount of DOX released in the dissolution medium was quantified using a validated spectrophotometric method. The total DOX released was calculated as a percentage of the theoretical maximum DOX content value.

Cells were cultured in 25 cm2 cell culture flasks. L929 and MDA-MB-231 (both from ATCC through LGC Promochem, London, UK) were grown with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) (PAN-Biotech, Aidenbach, Germany) medium with 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (GRISP, Porto, Portugal) and 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, PAN-Biotech). BJ-5ta cells (LGC Promochem) were grown in a 4:1 mixture of DMEM and Medium 199 (PAN-Biotech), supplemented with 1 % penicillin/streptomycin, 10 % FBS and 0.01 mg/mL of hygromycin B (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). MCF10A cells (LGC Promochem) were cultured in a 1:1 mixture of DMEM and F12 medium (PAN-Biotech) supplemented with 5 % horse serum, 20 ng/mL of epidermal growth factor (EGF), 0.5 μg/mL of hydrocortisone, 100 ng/mL of cholera toxin and 10 μg/mL of insulin (all supplements from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). Cells were kept in a humidified incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2) (Heracell 150i, Thermo Fisher Scientific) and sub-cultured every 3–4 days, upon 80−90 % confluence, by using 0.05 % trypsin (Sigma-Aldrich).

The day before starting the experiments, 5 × 103 (L929), 1 × 104 (BJ-5ta), 1.5 × 104 (MDA-MB-231) and 1.2 × 104 (MCF10A) cells were seeded per well in 96-well plates. Cells were incubated with the different conjugates with corresponding DOX concentration ranging from 0.30 to 5.40 μM. Life control was prepared with cells maintained in complete culture medium, and 30 % (v:v) DMSO (Sigma-Aldrich) was used to kill cells as death control. After 24, 48 and 72 h of incubation, MTT was performed, while Hoechst assay was done after 24 and 48 h of incubation.

After incubation, cell culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed with heated non-sterile PBS 1 × . Subsequently, 100 μL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT reagent (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide) (Sigma-Aldrich) was added per well and the plates were placed in a humidified incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2) for 2 h. Formazan crystals were solubilized with ethanol:DMSO (1:1) and absorbance at 570 nm was measured in a microplate reader (iMark™ Microplate Absorbance Reader, Bio-Rad, Hercules, USA).

After culture medium removal and wash with heated non-sterile PBS 1 × , 5 μg/mL Hoechst 34580 (Sigma-Aldrich) was added to the cells. The plates were maintained at room temperature, protected from light, for 40 min. After another wash with PBS 1 × , images were acquired in a fluorescence inverted microscope (350 nm excitation and 445 nm emission) (Olympus IX71, Tokyo, Japan). Cell nuclei were counted using ImageJ software version 1.53k (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, USA).

L929 cells were maintained and seeded as explained above. The highest concentration used in the previous assays (5.40 μM DOX and the corresponding 67.50 × 10−5 mg/mL CQD or NGO) were used to study cell internalization of all conjugates and individual constituents.

DOX, CQD, NGO or conjugates were added to the cells, which were then maintained in a humidified incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2) for 4 h. After incubation, culture medium was removed, and substituted by heated non-sterile PBS 1 × , to allow photography in a fluorescence inverted microscope (Olympus IX71), detecting TRITC (excitation at 544 nm; emission at 570 nm). Fluorescence intensity was determined in ImageJ software.

FET test with zebrafish embryos was used to evaluate toxicity of the CQD/NGO@DOX conjugates, according with the OECD test guideline (TG) 236 [33], using the same concentration range as in in cell viability assays. In this approach, newly fertilized zebrafish eggs were exposed to the conjugates for up to 72 h post fertilization (hpf). Three time points were established for assessment of measurements and developmental markers: In all time points, mortality and heart rate were monitored. At 32 hpf, eye, lens, yolk and chorion areas and spontaneous movements; at 56 hpf, head-trunk (H-T) angle, yolk extension and hatching; and at 72 hpf, embryonic length and survival.

In 24-well plates, 10 embryos at the same stage of embryo development were carefully selected per condition, while 20 embryos were used as controls. All colloidal dispersions were prepared in water, which was the control solvent. At 32 and 56 hpf, the colloidal dispersions on the wells were refreshed. Images were recorded with a Leica DM IL LED inverted microscope coupled with an ICC50 HD camera (Leica Biosystems, Nussloch, Germany). Different measurements were calculated using ImageJ software.
In a different subset of embryos, those were exposed to 5 μM DOX during 72 h, to study its biodistribution.

NGO were evaluated in terms of size, surface charge (estimated by ZP) and morphology. The post-production method of extrusion through polycarbonate filters with successively smaller pore sizes succeeded in achieving a more homogeneous population with smaller NGO sheets (108 ± 49 nm as determined for aqueous suspensions of NGO by DLS, Fig. 1 A or 177 ± 49 nm as determined for dried NGO by SEM, Fig. 1B), when compared to non-extruded NGO (339 ± 265 nm as determined for aqueous suspensions of NGO by DLS, Fig. 1A or 393 ± 145 nm as determined for dried NGO by SEM, Fig. 1B). Concerning ZP, aqueous suspensions of either non-extruded or extruded NGO exhibit a slightly negative surface charge, with a marginal increase in module as size decreases (Fig. 1A). Low ZP is associated with aggregation phenomena through π‒π stacking interactions between the aromatic carbon graphene backbones of NGO. However, these week interactions can easily be disrupted through ultrasound exposure. The morphology of non-extruded (Fig. 1C) and extruded NGO (Fig. 1D) was assessed by SEM, which revealed a flaked-like structure of stacked graphene nanosheets. Fig. 1D confirms the increased homogeneity and the smaller sizes of the extruded NGO.

NGO have a maximum emission wavelength at 530 nm (Fig. 2B–Table 1) consistent with its bright green emission (Fig. 2B – inset photograph) when excited with blue light (461 nm) in contrast with the blue fluorescence emitted by CQD (435 nm) (Table 1 and Fig. 3 ). Previous reports have suggested that the photoluminescence of NGO also originates from the surface defects that include oxygen-containing groups (defect state emission) among which the carboxyl groups that contribute to green emission, while the hydroxyl groups contribute to blue emission [43,46,47]. Thereby NGO surface must be richer in carboxyl groups.

Although near infrared (NIR) probes are ordinarily preferred for tissue penetration, green fluorescence probes have also been widely used to image tumors on internal organs [48]. In this regard, compared to CQD, the green emission of NGO has more advantages, being less energetic, providing less damage in healthy tissue and having higher depth of penetration. Additionally, a stable emission is observed independently of the NGO being extruded through different pore size filters (Fig. 2B, superimposed emission spectra obtained for NGO extruded trough 50, 100, 200 and 400). Furthermore, the NGO extruded emission profile remained steady for at least 12 months, indicating remarkable photostability.

The values of QY % of NGO are lower (19.69 %) when compared with CQD (67.65 %) (Table 1), but the QY % of graphene-based nanomaterials without surface functionalization are typically low [49,50]. The smaller Stokes shift observed for the NGO produced (Table 1) indicates that the excitation and energy relaxation processes are localized in the same site, ruling out the possibility of inter-site charge and energy transfer effects and assigns the emitting site to isolated sp2 clusters within the graphene backbone [44]. Finally, the Full Width Half Maximum (FWHM) was determined, indicating an NGO emission band comparable with other reported GO based materials [44], but broader than the CQD that present a smaller FWHM (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH impact on NGO surface functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of pH on NGO surface functional groups was investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy and ELS, and the results were compared to those obtained for CQD (Fig. 3A and B).

Fig. 3A shows that the pH-dependent fluorescence emission wavelength of NGO and CQD has inflection points that delimit regions assigned to different ionization of their surface functional groups. The deprotonation of carboxyl groups (COOH, region 1 of the pH range) into the carboxylate moieties (COO−, region 2 of the pH range) is most likely the cause of the inflection point observed for the pH-dependent fluorescence of CQD (Fig. 3A, blue symbols). This is confirmed by ELS, which shows that at roughly the same pH, the ZP decreases due to an increase in negatively charged carboxylate groups on the CQD surface (Fig. 3B blue symbols). The NGO ionization pattern is more complex, with two inflection points observed in the fluorescence emission wavelength, indicating the presence of more chemical functional groups distributed across three distinct pH regions (Fig. 3A, green symbols and pH regions 1–3). The first inflection in fluorescence maximum wavelength (Fig. 3A, green symbols) corresponds to the ionization of carboxyl groups (region 1) into carboxylate groups (region 2). The second increase in maximum emission wavelength (Fig. 3A, green symbols) is due to the ionization of the aromatic hydroxyl groups (phenol groups), which become negatively charged (region 3). These results agree with the reported pH-dependence of graphite oxide fluorescence [51]. In line with this observation, ZP values decrease with pH (Fig. 3B, green symbols), indicating that NGO becomes more negatively charged in region 2 due to the presence of carboxylate groups, and even more so in region 3 due to the presence of deprotonated aromatic phenol groups. The magnitude of ZP is an indicator of colloidal stability. A ZP of ±30 mV is ideal to guarantee adequate dispersion of these nanosystems [52,53]. Consequently, the colloidal stability of NGO increases with pH and is significantly higher than that of CQD at pH > 9, providing long shelf stability if stored in buffered solutions at this pH. The pH impact on the surface functional groups and charge of NGO is greater than that of CQD, which is an advantage for drug conjugation and pH-triggered drug release, as discussed in the following sections. Indeed, Fig. 3C depicts a schematization of NGO surface functional groups and their pH dependence, revealing that at very acidic pH (region 1), the carboxylic and phenol groups are protonated, whereas in region 2 carboxylate groups confer a negatively charged surface reinforced by the deprotonated phenol at higher pH values (region 3).

The chemical functional groups composition of NGO and CQD was also evaluated through ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S2 – supplementary materials). Both carbon-based nanosystems have similar surface groups like the hydroxyl (broad peak at 3050–3800 cm−1), and carbonyl (CO) from carboxyl or carboxylate groups (1250–1700 cm−1) [51,54–56]. In the case of NGO, the broader band between 2860 cm−1 and 3800 cm−1 indicates the presence of aliphatic and aromatic C–H and O–H stretch vibrations and small bands between 1000 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1 can be assigned to hydroxyl and epoxy groups [51,54,57]. Among the assigned infrared bands, one of the most recognizable is the carbonyl stretching band, whose frequency varies greatly depending on the chemical surroundings. In agreement with this, Fig. 3D presents a close-up of the ATR-FTIR spectra of NGO at two pH values (pH 5.5 and 7.4), showing the symmetric and antisymmetric infrared stretching modes of carboxylic and carboxylate moieties at around 1400 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. As the pH rises, deprotonation of the carboxyl groups causes a red shift (deviation to higher wavelengths or lower wavenumbers) in the stretching vibrations, indicating the conversion into carboxylate groups, as observed by fluorescence and ELS (Fig. 3A and B, green symbols) and consistent with the literature [55].

Unlike other drug delivery systems, NGO stands out as an exceptional nanocarrier because both surfaces of the graphene oxide sheet are available for drug conjugation. The π‒electrons present in the graphitic domains facilitate noncovalent interactions, such as π‒π stacking, with various molecules, including DOX [11,58,59]. These interactions allow DOX to be immobilized on the NGO plane through noncovalent physical adsorption. Additionally, the functional groups on the surface of NGO, which ionize depending on the pH, enable further DOX binding through electrostatic interactions [60–63], expanding its versatility as a drug carrier. After identification of the chemical functional groups on NGO surface and their pH-dependent ionization, the optical and surface properties of DOX and NGO conjugates (NGO@DOX) were characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy and ELS (Fig. 4 A and B).

In absorbance spectroscopy, it is known that when two components in a mixture do not interfere with one another, the absorption of light by the components is additive; that is, the total absorption of light at any given wavelength is simply the sum of the absorbance that the two components would show if measured individually under the same conditions [64]. The UV–Vis spectra of free NGO (green spectrum) and DOX (red spectrum), as well as the sum of these two spectra (NGO + DOX dashed purple spectrum) or the spectrum of their conjugates (NGO@DOX purple spectrum), are shown in Fig. 4A. If no conjugates were formed between DOX and NGO, the absorption spectrum of the mixture of the two would simply reflect the addition of their individual absorption spectra (NGO + DOX would be superimposed to NGO@DOX). However, the spectrum of the NGO@DOX is completely different from the additive spectrum and shows a red shift compared to the spectrum of free DOX. This shift, observed in other reports [61,63], might be due to the ground-state electron donor‒acceptor interaction between DOX and NGO which suggests that DOX was successfully loaded onto NGO. Moreover, DOX and NGO are expected to form π‒π stacking interactions, which can influence the absorption spectrum of the binding molecules. This interaction often results in a decrease in absorption intensity, known as hypochromic effect, as observed in Fig. 4A (DOX maximum absorbance in the red spectrum, decreases upon conjugation in NGO@DOX purple spectrum). In the literature, the hypochromic effect is attributed to weak dipole–dipole interactions between π‒π stacked chromophores [65].

The ZP variations (ΔZP) upon adding increasing concentrations of DOX to a fixed concentration of NGO (Fig. 4B) or CQD (Fig. S3, supplementary materials) were evaluated at physiological pH (pH 7.4) and endosomal pH of tumor cells (pH 5.5) and results fit to a saturation binding plot supporting that DOX conjugation with NGO is also electrostatically driven. From Fig. 4 and S3 it was possible to calculate and compare the dissociation constant Kd of the NGO@DOX and CQD@DOX at both pH values (Fig. 4C). Fig. 4C shows that the dissociation of the CQD@DOX (blue bars) is significantly lower than that of the NGO@DOX (green bars), and there are no significant pH differences for CQD conjugates. These findings support a key advantage of NGO as DOX nanocarriers over CQD: CQD exhibit lower drug release capacity and DOX release from NGO can be triggered at the more acidic tumor sites. There are two primary explanations for the higher DOX release at pH 5.5 and the relatively low DOX release at pH 7.4. At pH 5.5, DOX exhibits increased hydrophilicity and solubility. In a low-pH environment (pH 5.5), the drug is almost completely protonated and thus water soluble, whereas at higher physiological pH (pH 7.4), the DOX protonated fraction decreases to 40 %, with the remainder in the zwitterionic state, making it less water-soluble [27,63]. The other reason is the NGO's pH-dependent degree of ionization. Indeed, as shown in Fig. 3, at pH 5.5, NGO's carboxylic surface group and aromatic phenol groups are still nearly fully protonated. As a result, NGO's surface charge is close to neutral, and there is less electrostatic interaction between NGO and DOX. As pH rises, NGO's carboxylic groups become negatively charged, which increases the coulombic attraction of DOX's positively charged amino groups [60,61]. Additionally, as pH increases, the phenol group dissociates, enhancing the π‒π interaction between NGO and DOX [60,61]. Although CQD contain carboxylic ionizable surface groups, they have less pH dependency and surface charge amplitude than NGO, as shown in Fig. 3B, confirming the absence of pH influence in CQD dissociation (Fig. 4B, blue bars).

NGO's drug loading (Fig. 4D, green bars) is also higher than that of CQD (Fig. 4D, blue bars) for both pH values tested meaning that the amount of DOX loaded per unit weight of NGO is higher comparatively to the amount of drug loaded in CQD or other reported graphene based nanosystems which present DL% of 0.08–37.6 % [66–71]. Differences in Kd and DL% are thus related to the different surface chemistry of the nanomaterials, which result in different DOX binding and release dynamics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro studies for predicting therapeutic potential

ce:section.ce:para: The biophysical characteristics of membranes, such as their lipid packing and order, as well as their lipid conformation, can be altered by interactions between drugs or drug delivery systems and membranes. Thus, examining these interactions can provide important insights into cellular uptake capacity and consequently, therapeutic activity. If the membrane biophysical impairment is noticeable, it may also be connected to the cytotoxicity of the nanosystems [72–74]. In this regard, the interaction of DOX, NGO, CQD, and their conjugates (NGO@DOX or CQD@DOX) with lipid membranes were evaluated by SAXS to obtain information on the bilayer structure and lipid packing (Fig. 5 ). DPPC multi-stacked bilayers were chosen as a membrane model and SAXS measurements were performed at 50 °C to mimic the membrane fluid phase at pH 5.5 (Fig. 5A), representative of the acidic microenvironment of tumors and lysosomes and pH 7.4 (Fig. 5B), representative of blood and healthy tissues [25–28].

The bilayer repeat distance (d values correspondent to bilayer thickness, including a water layer between the bilayers) and the correlation length (ξ values related to the full width of the peaks at one-half of their intensity) were determined from the SAXS patterns of DPPC membrane model systems (Fig. 5), and the values are presented in Table 2 .

At acidic pH, the addition of free DOX, NGO, CQD or CQD/@DOX to DPPC resulted in a significant increase in the d values (p ≤ 0.001). The increase in d values indicates that these additives permeate the membrane and change the area requirement of the membrane phospholipid headgroups, allowing the chains to lose their tilt and extend to accommodate the additives [73–75]. These changes are more evident upon CQD or CQD@DOX addition to the membrane model and are corroborated by significant reduction of the correlation length. At neutral pH only the addition of CQD or CQD@DOX resulted in a significant increase in the d values (p ≤ 0.001) again corroborated by a significant reduction of the multilamellar correlation length which suggests a higher disruption effect of CQD on the membrane structure [73]. In the case of NGO, the differences observed in membrane permeation may be attributed to their pH-dependent ionization. At acidic pH, NGO are protonated, resulting in lower surface charge and solubility in water, while increasing solubility in the lipid phase of the membrane. As pH increases, the ionization of functional groups results in greater negative charges, which enhance water solubility and decrease the membrane permeation of NGO. The membrane permeation of CQD occurred at both pH values, indicating that CQD exhibits lower pH dependency and surface charge amplitude compared to NGO, as illustrated in Figs. 3B and 4B.

Biological membranes possess dynamic endocytic and active transport mechanisms that facilitate the internalization of nanostructures and drugs. Thus, while NGO may initially interact with and cause changes in membrane biophysics, this does not rule out subsequent translocation processes. The observed interactions highlight NGO's ability to interact with lipid bilayers, consistent with the behavior of therapeutic agents.

Stimuli-triggered drug release is one of the strategies being used for the specific delivery of anticancer drugs to target tumor tissues while reducing drug distribution in non-target healthy tissues [76]. Here, we developed an approach that takes advantage of the acidic pH found in tumor microenvironments as a stimulus for DOX release, whilst in physiological pH, DOX remains bound to the NGO.
DOX release profile was evaluated at 5.5 and 7.4 pH values, that mimic the tumor microenvironment and the physiological pH, respectively (Fig. 6 A).

By comparing the final cumulative DOX release of 99 ± 1 % and 33 ± 1 % in 24 h at pH values of 5.5 and 7.4 (Fig. 6 A), respectively, it can be concluded that acidic conditions accelerate DOX release, which is in good agreement with previous reports [77–79]. This can be understood by looking at the interactions between DOX and NGO and how they are affected by pH changes. At a low pH (5.5), DOX is mostly positively charged making it more soluble in water. At the same pH, the carboxylic and aromatic phenol groups on NGO are mostly protonated, resulting in a nearly neutral surface. Because of this, there is little to no electrostatic attraction between NGO and DOX, making it easier for the drug to dissociate from NGO, as shown by the higher Kd value of NGO@DOX at pH 5.5 (Fig. 4C). As the pH increases, the carboxylic groups on NGO become negatively charged, increasing the electrostatic attraction to DOX's positively charged amino groups. This also strengthens the π-π interaction between NGO and DOX, making the drug less likely to dissociate from NGO at pH 7.4 (Fig. 4C). While the association and dissociation of DOX with NGO are governed by similar pH-dependent processes, the release kinetics differ significantly, as highlighted by the Weibull kinetic fitting model. At pH 5.5, the release is more sustained, reflected by the "a" parameter, which represents the time at which 63.2 % of the maximum drug release is achieved. This parameter is approximately double at pH 7.4 compared to pH 5.5, indicating that 63.2 % of the maximum drug is released in 3.36 h (i.e. 0.14 of 24h) at pH 5.5 versus 5.52 h (i.e. 0.23 of 24h) at pH 7.4.

These findings indicate the potential of utilizing pH as an internal trigger for NGO to effectively facilitate DOX release. It is reasonable to assume that NGO@DOX nanoassembly is stable in blood circulation and at normal tissues (pH 7.4) until it reaches the tumor microenvironment. Upon entering the cell, the acidic environment of endosomes and lysosomes (pH 5.5) facilitates the release of the drug from the nanoassembly. However, the in vitro demonstration of a pH-triggered release, while plausible, requires further verification in vivo.

As shown in Fig. 6B (NGO before conjugation), and in agreement with the literature [61,80–82] two bands are observed in the Raman spectrum of NGO: the G band at 1593 cm−1, related to in-plane vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice, and the D band at 1341 cm−1, related to the presence of sp3 defects associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered carbons. The DOX Raman spectrum, presented in Fig. 6B, exhibits some noise and limited band resolution due to an intense fluorescence background that required correction. Notably, it reveals spectral fingerprint bands of the drug at 1244, 1412, 1442 and 1580 cm−1 from which the most intense one at 1412 cm−1 has been attributed to the phenyl ring vibration, consistent with previously published DOX Raman spectra [83,84]. NGO@DOX possess bands from both DOX and NGO, but conjugation has led to diminishing intensity of the vibrational modes in agreement with what has been observed for other conjugated systems [85]. The exposure of NGO@DOX conjugates to increased laser potency led to the complete disappearance of the bands associated with DOX, accompanied by a notable enhancement of the D and G bands characteristic of NGO. The observed phenomenon likely arises from the release of DOX from the conjugates, triggered by a photothermic effect that disrupts the π-π stacking interaction. Extended exposure time to laser produced a similar release effect, indicating the controlled release of DOX from gold nanocages due to the photothermic effect, as demonstrated by Raman spectroscopy [86]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the characteristic D and G bands serves as a correlation for the structural properties of carbon, with this ratio being inversely proportional to the in-plane graphitic domain size (crystalline grains), when described for nanocrystalline graphite [80,87]. The ID/IG ratio, determined by fitting the Raman spectra with Lorentzian functions, of the NGO Raman spectrum before conjugation with DOX (Fig. 6 B top left Raman spectrum) was 0.99, whereas lower ID/IG ratios were reported for graphite (ca. 0.82) [88]. This indicates that NGO exhibits greater defects compared to graphite sheets, likely attributable to the predominant influence of edge states at the periphery of NGO. Moreover, following the release of DOX from the conjugates (Fig. 6B, NGO after DOX release), their ID/IG ratio decreased to 0.79, likely due to the intense photothermic effect.

With L929 cells, the MTT results regarding conjugates with CQD (Fig. 7A–C) showed that only the highest tested concentration negatively affects cell viability, after 72 h of exposure (Fig. 7C). For the conjugates with NGO (Fig. 7D–F), cell viability decreased with highest concentrations (Fig. 7E) after 48 and 72h.

Overall, MTT results demonstrate that NGO conjugates are more toxic than CQD. Since neither CQD nor NGO affected cell viability, the observed differences might arise from the different surface chemistry of the nanosystems, and consequently their interaction with DOX and its release from the conjugates, which is represented by the higher Kd of NGO when compared with CQD (Fig. 4C). Since DOX is more easily released from NGO@DOX conjugates it can exert its recognized cytotoxicity.

The reduction in cell number is more pronounced than changes in metabolic viability, according to the comparison between the results of MTT and Hoechst assays. Interestingly, while cell metabolism starts to significantly decrease after 48 h of incubation with NGO conjugates and after 72 h for CQD conjugates, the total cell number suffers a sharp decrease already at 24 h, except with the lowest concentration of CQD@DOX tested (Fig. 7G). Furthermore, after a 24 h incubation, the biggest noticeable impact was obtained with NGO@DOX conjugates (Fig. 7I), when compared with CQD@DOX (Fig. 7G). After 48 h of exposure, the impact for either type of conjugate is similar. Curiously, NGO alone promoted an increase in cell number when compared with life control, which is not observed with CQD only, hinting for the possibility that NGO impacts cellular events such as proliferation and metabolism [89–92]. This hypothesis would have to be verified by further studies and is not within the scope of the current work.

Taken together, it appears that after a 24 h-incubation, the number of cells diminishes, and the surviving cells partly compensate for the metabolic activity. MTT conversion to formazan is catalyzed by mitochondrial dehydrogenases meaning that is dependent on mitochondrial respiration and indirectly show the energetic status of the cells [89]. DOX is known to inhibit macromolecular biosynthesis and killing fast-growing cells [8,9]. Therefore, it is possible that even though cell numbers decrease rapidly after DOX exposure, cells try to compensate with a more intense metabolic activity. These results highlight the importance of using more than one method to assess toxicity, as it may give a limited and erroneous perspective.

DOX exerts its cellular effect through two main mechanisms of action: (1) DNA intercalation and the disruption of the activity of DNA topoisomerase II; and (2) generation of free radicals that ultimately damage DNA, membranes, and proteins [93]. As a free drug, DOX enters the cells and accumulates in the nucleus [94,95]. The cellular localization of CQD after entering the cell depends on their surface coating, mostly localizing in the nucleus as reported for GQD synthesized by green processes in several cell lines including L929 [96].

Fluorescence intensity of free DOX and CQD/NGO@DOX was evaluated in L929 cell line (Fig. 7K). The conjugation between either CQD or NGO and DOX, and consequent proximity between the moieties, allows the transference of energy from a donor to an acceptor fluorophore [97]. Fig. 7K clearly shows that both conjugates exhibit significant more intense fluorescence emission. Consequently, both conjugates serve as theranostic tools by combining the therapeutic effect of DOX with an increased fluorescent signal useful for imaging applications.

To draw a more realistic cytotoxicity profile of the conjugates, MTT assay was also performed in human cell lines, namely BJ-5ta (normal skin), MCF10A (normal breast) and MDA-MB-231 (triple negative breast cancer). Results are depicted in Table 3 (and Fig. S4 as supplementary materials).

The impact of conjugates on BJ-5ta and MCF10A cell lines reveals distinct effects on cell viability, highlighting the role of CQD@DOX, NGO@DOX, and free DOX. In BJ-5ta cells, CQD@DOX significantly reduced cell viability starting from the 48 h mark across all tested concentrations. NGO@DOX and free DOX exhibited more approximate trends, affecting cell viability at all time points and concentrations. This aligns with prior findings indicating that CQD has a greater resistance to dissociate from DOX. Notably, both free CQD and NGO did not adversely affect cell viability. In MCF10A cells, the conjugation with CQD also impairs DOX cytotoxicity but more slightly than compared with the results from BJ-5ta cell line. In contrast, both CQD and NGO treatment lead to a gradual decrease in cell viability. This comparison underscores how distinct conjugates can differentially modulate drug effects in different cell lines.

In breast cancer cells, NGO@DOX treatment caused a similar pattern of cytotoxicity as in the normal cell lines. CQD@DOX treatment leads to a decrease in cell viability at the 24 h-incubation period when higher concentrations are used but, at 48 h, the impact fades. Free CQD does not have such an impact on cell viability and only the higher concentration of CQD@DOX leads to a decrease, free NGO does not cause cytotoxicity and free DOX impacts viability cumulatively over time, as expected [98–100].
The encapsulation of these conjugates in nanocarriers might mitigate the impact on healthy cells, and possibly improve the cytotoxic effect in cancer cells [101].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo studies for predicting therapeutic potential
ce:section.ce:para: During the fish embryo acute toxicity (FET) test, recommended by the OECD, a series of observations and measurements regarding zebrafish development were recorded. Hatching and spontaneous movement are good indicators of appropriate neuromotor development, while embryo length, chorion, yolk sac, eye and lens area, head-trunk angle and yolk extension correlate with proper metabolism and normal progression of embryonic development [33].

Embryo survival and heart rate monitored throughout the experiment are presented in Fig. 8 . Results on eye, lens, yolk and chorion areas, spontaneous movements, H-T angle, yolk extension, hatching and embryonic length and survival are depicted in Fig. S5, supplementary materials.

Zebrafish are currently considered a good vertebrate model for toxicological assays [33,102,103]. Cardiotoxicity of DOX is well documented in the literature [104] and recent studies in embryo and adult zebrafish reveal DOX-induced changes in blood circulation and cardiac function, specifically a decreased heart rate when compared with the control [105,106].

CQD@DOX (Fig. 8A) did not affect heart rate, which was not the case for NGO@DOX (Fig. 8C). NGO@DOX induced cardiotoxicity even at low concentrations. Curiously, for the highest concentration tested, NGO conjugates (Fig. 8C) led to heart rate increase. Usually, DOX is described to decrease heart rate in a dose-dependent manner [107], although increased heart rate is also reported as a sign of DOX's toxicity [108].

Survival data (Fig. 8B and D) did not follow a clear trend as tested concentrations increased. This might be due to aggregation phenomena, which was clearly visible to the naked eye. Zebrafish chorion has pores with ≈0.6–0.7 μm size [109] which can be affected by nanoparticle size and aggregation. These pores are pivotal for nutrient and oxygen transportation to the embryo and their removal. Interferences with these phenomena result in toxicity to the embryos. Therefore, for certain CQD/NGO@DOX concentrations tested, similar effects might occur and explain the mortality observed [110–112].
Once again, conjugates with CQD or NGO lead to different outcomes in terms of DOX toxicity which are probably related with different DOX release dynamics, as discussed before.

After 72 h of exposure to 5 μM DOX, DOX biodistribution was verified by taking advantage of its intrinsic fluorescence (Fig. 8E–G). Fluorescence shows that DOX is properly internalized by zebrafish embryos and accumulates in the yolk, where hydrophobic molecules such as the chromophore region of DOX molecule can accumulate [113].

NGO was synthesized, thoroughly characterized and successfully conjugated with DOX. CQD were used as a comparison. Both CQD and NGO have a negative surface charge due to the functional groups rich in oxygen which can be used to conjugate DOX at certain pH values, generating a pH-triggered delivery nanosystem. These conjugates remain bound in physiological conditions, but not in the characteristic acidic pH of cancer tissues. NGO emits green fluorescence whilst CQD emits blue. Green fluorescence has advantages for imaging tumors in internal organs. NGO are photostable and possess a lower QY% comparatively to CQD but are comparable to similar graphene-based nanomaterials. However, NGO's dissociation constant and drug loading are higher. Besides, NGO and NGO@DOX promote less membrane destabilization in comparison with their CQD counterparts.

The toxicity profile demonstrates that NGO@DOX are more toxic than CQD@DOX, not because of the NGO itself, but because of differences in the DOX release profile due to differences in surface chemistry, DL% and Kd between NGO and CQD. This is reflected in reduced cell viability in vitro and cardiotoxicity in vivo. Both CQD and NGO were cytocompatible except in the MCF10A cell line, which is particularly sensitive in our studies. DOX, CQD@DOX and NGO@DOX permeate the cells and accumulate in the nucleus exhibiting higher fluorescence intensity conjugated comparatively to free DOX suggesting the possibility of these conjugates to be used as promising nanomaterials in theranostic applications.
